macro_segments.title: Renting a Field: Changing Crops and the Dispute Between Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel and the Rabbis

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: Substituting crops (barley for wheat, cereals for legumes)
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's strict position: Contract terms must be honored
Rav Chisda's challenge: Why forbid a less damaging substitution?
The Torah principle: Jews must not lie; honoring contracts is divinely required
Meitivi: The Purim collections challenge—sometimes enforcement is lenient
Abaye's explanation: Rabban Shimon's concern about cumulative field damage over years
Agricultural practice: Plowing direction and crop rotation effects on soil

macro_segments.title: Cereals and Legumes: Regional Variation Between Eretz Yisrael and Bavel

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's teaching to Rabin: Opposite ruling in Bavel regarding cereals and legumes
Explanation via Nimukei Yosef: Rainfall vs. irrigation determines the rule

macro_segments.title: Garden Cress (Tachal) in Flax Fields: When Plant Interference Is Permitted and Prohibited

micro_segments.title: Once hardened with seeds, cress is protected even among flax (damage already done)
Rav Yehuda to Rabin bar Rav Nachman: Garden cress damages flax when growing among it

macro_segments.title: Trees on the Border Mound (Metzer): Ownership, Roots, and Fruit Division

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's saying: What's yours is mine and what's mine is yours (bordering orchards)

macro_segments.title: Garden Cress (Tachal) in Flax Fields: When Plant Interference Is Permitted and Prohibited (continued)

micro_segments.title: Exception: If cress is on field borders, it is prohibited to take it (intentional planting)

macro_segments.title: Trees on the Border Mound (Metzer): Ownership, Roots, and Fruit Division (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav vs. Shmuel: Rav says fruit follows roots; Shmuel says split the fruit (yachloku)
Meitivi: A baraita supports Shmuel—trees on the border must be split (yachloku)
Definition of metzer: A raised mound between fields; orchards planted upon it
Rashi's interpretation: Roots on one side, tree and fruit on the other side of the boundary
Tosafot's alternative: Metzer is a line with no width; roots exist in shared space
Targumo l'Shmuel aliba d'Rav: How Shmuel's position accommodates Rav's principle
Rav's exception: When the metzer is fully filled with roots on both sides
Cutting the tree: Horizontal vs. perpendicular cuts to achieve equal fruit distribution

macro_segments.title: Adjacent Property Concerns: Proximity to Cities and River Banks

micro_segments.title: Rav Gidal to Rav Nachman: Do not buy land adjacent to a city (safety and interference concerns)
Rav's principle: Standing in a neighbor's field at harvest time is forbidden
Rav Abba's interpretation of Deuteronomy blessing: Nearness to synagogue; cleanliness; fertility
Rabbi Yochanan's alternative interpretation: Proximity to sanitary facilities, not synagogue
Resolution: A wall around the city permits adjacent land; without a wall, it is problematic

macro_segments.title: Removing Illness: Multiple Rabbinic Interpretations of Deuteronomy 7:15

micro_segments.title: Rav's view: Choli refers to the evil eye (ayin ha-ra)
Shmuel's view: Choli refers to wind sickness (affecting all illnesses)
Rabbi Chanina's view: Choli refers to cold (tzina) and exposure
Rabbi Yosi bar Chanina's view: Choli refers to bodily excretions (nasal and ear)
Rabbi Elazar's view: Choli refers to bile (marah) and gallbladder function
Baraita support: Marah causes illness; 83 diseases stem from bile; bread and salt cure them

macro_segments.title: Pat Shacharit (Morning Bread): Thirteen Wonders of Eating Bread at Dawn

micro_segments.title: The thirteen benefits: Protection from sun, cold, wind, demons; wisdom; legal success; Torah study
Additional benefits: Spoken words heard; learning retained; no fever; marital fidelity; kills worms
Additional opinion: Morning bread removes jealousy and brings love
Rabbah to Rava bar Mari: Common saying—sixty runners cannot catch a man who ate morning bread
Rabbinic instruction: Eat in summer for heat protection; in winter for cold protection
Scriptural support: Isaiah 49:10—no hunger, no thirst, no heat, no sun damage
Rava's stronger source: Deuteronomy 28:5-6—serve God, bless bread and water, sickness removed
Prescription: Bread dipped in salt and a jug of water (pat bemelach v'kiton)

macro_segments.title: Surveying Land and Public River Access: Practical Applications of Land Law

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda to Rav Adda the surveyor: Do not neglect precision; every piece could grow saffron
Four amot around irrigation channels: Belong to field owners; less stringency required
River banks: Public domain; must not measure or neglect; belong to all users
Rabbi Ami's announcement: Clear vegetation for barge-pullers (two amot per side minimum)
Rav Natan bar Sharya's stringency: Cleared 16 amot; local residents objected and beat him
Distinction: Public domain (reshut harabim) is 16 amot; river access is by functional need
Rabba bar Rav Huna's case: Waited for neighbors to clear first before clearing his forest
Objection: Resh Lakish—adorn yourself first, then others; don't wait for others' compliance
Resolution: Rabba's neighbor was a general (Parzak) who refused; clearing alone was futile

topical_tags.term: Tree ownership and fruit division on shared border
Standing in neighbor's field at harvest
Rabba bar Rav Huna (forest and river)
